cards.title: Moral Luck
cards.body: Philosopher Bernard Williams introduced the concept of moral luck, which questions whether our moral judgments should be based on factors beyond our control.

cards.title: The Veil of Ignorance
cards.body: John Rawls' thought experiment proposes that we should design a just society from behind a veil of ignorance, without knowing our own social status or position.

cards.title: The Problem of Evil
cards.body: This philosophical problem questions how an all-powerful, all-knowing, and all-good God could allow evil to exist in the world.

cards.title: Epicureanism
cards.body: Epicureanism is a school of thought that advocates for the pursuit of moderate desires and the avoidance of physical pain and mental distress.

cards.title: Stoicism
cards.body: Stoicism is a philosophy that emphasizes reason, self-control, and indifference to external events, with the goal of achieving inner peace and wisdom.

cards.title: The Hard Problem of Consciousness
cards.body: Philosopher David Chalmers formulated the hard problem of consciousness, which questions why we have subjective experiences at all.

cards.title: Solipsism
cards.body: Solipsism is the philosophical idea that only one's own mind is sure to exist, and that the external world and other minds may not be real.

cards.title: Absurdism
cards.body: Absurdism is a philosophical and literary movement that emphasizes the absurdity and meaninglessness of human existence, often with a sense of irony or humor.

cards.title: Determinism
cards.body: Determinism is the philosophical idea that every event, including human decisions and actions, is the inevitable result of prior causes and is therefore predetermined.

cards.title: Existentialism
cards.body: Existentialism is a philosophical movement that emphasizes individual freedom and choice, and the inherent meaninglessness of life, which must be overcome by individual responsibility.
